target: data/sections.json part1 SEC-1.3 — How It Differs from the Globe Model: body expansion + TLDR fixes
replacement_html:
1.3 How It Differs from the Globe Model

The globe model's key structural predictions — oblate spheroid geometry, geodynamo-driven magnetic field, orbital mechanics (with relativistic corrections verified by GPS) — are independently confirmed by gravimetry, satellite observations, seismic tomography, and deep-space probes. The dome must either match or beat these predictions on their own terms. Where it borrows globe data (WGS-84 coordinates, IGRF magnetic field models) it is not competing — it is depending.

Mainstream cosmology describes Earth as an oblate spheroid (equatorial radius 6,378.1 km, polar radius 6,356.8 km) orbiting the Sun at approximately 150 million km. The geomagnetic field is generated by convective dynamics in the molten iron outer core — the geodynamo. The atmosphere transitions into a conductive ionosphere above ~60 km altitude. No physical dome or firmament exists. The globe model is supported by convergent independent evidence: satellite imagery, GPS navigation (requiring orbital mechanics with both special and general relativistic corrections — a 38 microsecond/day clock drift without them), deep-space probes, lunar laser ranging, Gaia astrometry of 1.8 billion stars, seismic tomography, and centuries of maritime navigation.

The dome model (described in Section 1.1) replaces these mechanisms with a flat elliptical disc, a conductive copper firmament, a local sun at 5,733 km altitude, and a local moon at 2,534 km. Where the globe derives its shape from gravitational self-compression and its field from core dynamics, the dome posits a fixed toroidal cavity with geometry defined by curve-fitting parameters. The structural question is not which model sounds more plausible, but which makes independent predictions verified by methods that share no common instrumentation.

A critical asymmetry emerges in the data pipeline. The dome model's own coordinate system takes Polaris elevation as its primary input — and Polaris elevation is geographic latitude in the globe model. The formula r(city) = solve: r × tan(polaris_elevation) = H(r) converts globe-derived measurements into dome coordinates. The dome's Open Problems page (OPEN-001) explicitly acknowledges this: "Dome-native coordinates without WGS84 (IN PROGRESS)." Until that open problem is solved, every dome distance calculation inherits its accuracy from the globe coordinate system it claims to replace. This is not a peripheral issue — it means the dome's distance-prediction WINs (Section 1.1) are testing whether globe-derived inputs can be curve-fitted to globe-derived outputs, which is circularity, not confirmation.
holes_addressed: MAJOR: TLDR/body disconnect — body now grounds the dependency argument with OPEN-001 and Polaris-as-latitude evidence
MODERATE: Section now actually compares the two models as the title promises, with a brief dome paragraph
MINOR: Keplerian → orbital mechanics with relativistic corrections (38 μs/day GPS drift)
MINOR: Ionosphere range fixed from 80-400 km to ~60 km
NOTE: JPL ephemerides already patched to IGRF by ISS-849 (confirmed in current text)
new_evidence_added: Dome OPEN-001 explicit WGS-84 dependency admission (raw-text/05-model.txt line 96)
Polaris elevation = globe latitude identity (dome formula from raw-text/04-coordinates.txt line 13)
GPS relativistic correction: 38 microseconds/day clock drift (well-established physics, strengthens the orbital mechanics claim)
Cross-domain instrumentation independence argument (GPS radio + gravimetry mass + seismology waves + Gaia optical — no shared systematic errors)

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome model is working on OPEN-001 to replace WGS-84 — so the dependency is temporary.
anticipated_objections.response: OPEN-001 has been listed since at least V50.6 and remains IN PROGRESS. Until it is solved, every distance WIN published uses globe-derived inputs. A temporary dependency that covers all existing results is functionally identical to a permanent one for the purpose of evaluating those results.

anticipated_objections.objection: Polaris elevation is an observable, not a globe measurement — anyone can measure it.
anticipated_objections.response: True, the raw angle is instrument-derived. But the dome model converts that angle to a radial distance using r × tan(elevation) = H(r). The resulting distance is then compared to "actual" distances — which come from WGS-84 databases. Both the input interpretation (elevation ≈ latitude) and the validation reference are globe-derived. Independent measurement of the angle does not make the full pipeline independent.

anticipated_objections.objection: This section is supposed to describe the globe model, not attack the dome.
anticipated_objections.response: The title is "How It Differs from the Globe Model" — a comparison. The original body described only the globe side with no comparison or contrast. The rewrite adds a brief dome paragraph and then identifies the most structurally significant difference: the data pipeline dependency. This is comparative, not adversarial.

kernel_of_truth: The dome model does attempt genuine quantitative prediction using real observational data (INTERMAGNET stations, Polaris measurements, tidal records). This ambition is unusual among alternative cosmology models and makes the dependency question meaningful — a model that made no quantitative predictions would have no dependency to critique. The dome author has correctly identified that any serious alternative cosmology must engage with actual measurements, not just philosophical arguments. The problem is that the measurement pipeline borrows from the framework it claims to replace.
note_to_decider: This is a full section replacement including the <details> wrapper. The TLDR was updated: (1) Keplerian → orbital mechanics with relativistic corrections, (2) WGS-84 gravity → WGS-84 coordinates (more precise — the dome borrows coordinate data, not gravity data per se), (3) JPL ephemerides already removed by ISS-849. The body adds two new paragraphs: a brief dome model comparison paragraph and a detailed dependency-grounding paragraph citing OPEN-001 and the Polaris-as-latitude identity. Closes ISS-850.
